description: The displayed image does not correspond to the target figure from the paper focusing on Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels. It shows a software user interface button labeled "Check for updates", with an icon composed of a red bookmark-shaped symbol inside an incomplete ring that is blue in the upper half and yellow in the lower half. No content related to the alloying effects, microstructure characteristics, mechanical properties, heat treatment results, or density performance of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels can be identified from this incorrectly loaded image, so no valid material science information relevant to the paper's research can be extracted from the current displayed content.

This figure illustrates the geometric dimensions of two standardized mechanical test specimens for Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steel property characterization. The first subplot shows a cylindrical tensile specimen with M12 threaded ends: it has a gauge section with 5 mm diameter and 30 mm length, R5 transition fillets, 13 mm long end clamping sections, 65 mm total length, and a 60° central positioning hole at each end. The second subplot presents a standard V-notched Charpy impact specimen with dimensions of 10 mm × 10 mm × 55 mm, and a 2 mm deep V-notch. These specimens are applied for room temperature tensile tests at a strain rate of 1.1×10^-3 s^-1 and -40℃ Charpy impact tests respectively, providing unified specimen specifications to guarantee repeatability and comparability of tested mechanical property results.

This figure presents two room-temperature engineering stress-strain curve sets of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels after 1050℃ solution treatment for 2 h. Subplot (a) shows curves for Fe30Mn11AlxC alloys with C contents of 0.6 wt%, 1.0 wt%, 1.2 wt%, and 1.5 wt%: yield strength increases from 542 MPa to 1125 MPa with rising C content, while total elongation decreases from ~60% to ~25%, accompanied by a transition from work hardening to work softening at higher C levels. Subplot (b) shows curves for FexMn11Al1C alloys with Mn contents of 20 wt%, 25 wt%, 30 wt%, and 34 wt%: yield strength decreases from 896 MPa to 401 MPa as Mn content increases, while total elongation rises from ~28.5% to ~60% with an elevated work hardening rate. The figure confirms that C and Mn alloying contents significantly regulate the tensile mechanical properties of the tested low-density steels, providing experimental basis for composition design to balance strength and ductility.

This figure presents engineering stress-strain curves of Fe30MnxAl1C low-density steels after two different solution treatment processes, with Al content x set as 6, 8, 10, 11, 12 wt% respectively. Subplot (c) shows samples solution treated at 1050℃ for 2h followed by water quenching, while subplot (d) shows samples solution treated at 1100℃ for 2h followed by water quenching. The y-axis is engineering stress in MPa, and the x-axis is engineering strain in %. As Al content increases from 6 wt% to 12 wt%, yield strength and ultimate tensile strength increase significantly, with the 12Al sample reaching ~950 MPa yield strength, while fracture elongation decreases sharply. Compared with 1050℃ treated samples, 1100℃ treated counterparts exhibit slightly lower yield strength and higher fracture elongation, which is attributed to grain coarsening at higher solution temperature. These results reveal the regulation effects of Al content and solution temperature on tensile properties of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels, providing experimental support for alloy composition and process optimization.

This figure presents the microstructures of Fe30Mn11Al(x)C low-density steels with carbon contents x=0.6wt%, 1.0wt%, 1.2wt%, 1.5wt% after 1050 °C solution treatment for 2 h followed by water quenching. Subfigures (a)-(d) are EBSD maps: black lines represent grain boundaries with misorientation θ>15°, red lines are Σ3 twin boundaries, and green regions refer to BCC ferrite phase. The 0.6%C sample (a) contains ~30% BCC ferrite and finer austenite grains, while (b)-(d) for higher C contents are single FCC austenite with coarser grains, and twin boundary fraction decreases from ~60% to ~50% as C content rises. Subfigures (e)-(h) are dark-field TEM images with inset SAED patterns (taken along [001]γ zone axis) showing κ-carbides, whose size increases from ~1 nm (0.6%C) to ~80 nm (1.5%C), with regular rectangular morphology observed in 1.5%C sample following orientation relationship (001)κ || (001)γ. This figure demonstrates that C content regulates phase constitution, grain size, twin density and κ-carbide size, and a small amount of BCC phase effectively refines austenite grains.

This figure illustrates the microstructure of Fe(x)Mn11Al1C low-density steels with Mn content of 20 wt%, 25 wt%, 30 wt%, and 35 wt%, all subjected to 1050 °C solution treatment for 2 h followed by water quenching. Subfigures (a) to (d) are EBSD maps with a 200 μm scale bar: black lines denote high-angle grain boundaries (misorientation θ > 15°), red lines denote Σ3 twin boundaries, and green regions denote BCC ferrite phase. With increasing Mn content, ferrite volume fraction reduces from 3.3% (20Mn sample) to 0.65% (25Mn sample), and vanishes in 30Mn and 35Mn samples which exhibit single austenite phase; austenite grain size increases sequentially from 9.9 μm to 18.8 μm, 23.7 μm and 33 μm, while twin boundary fraction stays around 60% for all samples. Subfigures (e) to (h) are dark-field TEM images (scale bars: 100 nm for e,f, 20 nm for g,h) with inset SAED patterns collected along [001]γ zone axis, showing nano-sized κ-carbide precipitates. The average size of κ-carbides decreases from 18 nm (20Mn) to 5 nm (25Mn), 3 nm (30Mn) and 2 nm (35Mn) as Mn content rises. The figure proves that increasing Mn content suppresses ferrite formation, coarsens austenite grains, and refines κ-carbide precipitates in this alloy system.

This figure characterizes the microstructure of Fe30Mn(x)Al1C low-density steels with Al contents of 8 wt%, 10 wt%, 11 wt%, and 12 wt% after 1100 °C solution treatment for 2 h followed by water quenching. Subfigures (a)-(d) are EBSD maps: black lines represent high-angle grain boundaries with misorientation θ>15°, red lines represent Σ3 twin boundaries, and green lines in (d) (12 wt% Al sample) mark BCC ferrite phase. The average austenite grain size decreases from ~28.3 μm (8% Al), ~29.7 μm (10% Al), ~23.7 μm (11% Al) to ~12.6 μm (12% Al), with 3.1 vol% BCC phase only detected in the 12% Al sample. Subfigure (e) is a bright-field TEM image with an inset SAED pattern of the 8% Al sample, confirming no κ-carbide precipitates. Subfigures (f)-(h) are dark-field TEM images (acquired along the [001]γ zone axis using κ-carbide diffraction spots from the inset SAED patterns) for 10%, 11%, 12% Al samples respectively, showing uniform nanoscale κ-carbide precipitation, whose average diameter increases from ~1 nm (10% Al), ~1.5 nm (11% Al) to ~15 nm (12% Al). This figure demonstrates that Al content significantly regulates phase composition, grain size, and κ-carbide precipitation behavior in the studied steel system.

This figure demonstrates the alloying effects of C, Al, and Mn on the microstructure and density of Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steels after solution heat treatment at 1050 °C for 2 h followed by water quenching, consisting of two fitted scatter subplots. Subplot (a) plots average κ-carbide diameter (d_Car, unit: nm) against element mass percentage, where C content values are scaled 10 times for aligned display: both Al content (white star markers) and C content (red square markers) show strong positive linear correlation with κ-carbide size, while Mn content (blue circle markers) shows weak negative linear correlation with κ-carbide size, with linear least-squares fitting lines for each element. Subplot (b) plots mass density (ρ, unit: g/cm³) against element mass percentage: all three elements reduce density linearly with increasing content, and the derived linear fitting formula for density is ρ = 8.1 - 0.102C% - 0.1Al% - 0.01Mn%. The figure confirms that C and Al are the key elements regulating κ-carbide size and alloy density, providing quantitative guidance for low-density steel composition design.

This group of plots demonstrates the dependence of mechanical properties on the square root of κ-carbide size ($d_{Car}^{0.5}$) in solution-treated Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steels. Subplot (a) shows that both tensile strength (black squares) and yield strength (red circles) increase linearly with $d_{Car}^{0.5}$, with linear fitting formulas $R_m=786+54.6d_{Car}^{0.5}$ and $R_{p0.2}=478+97.1d_{Car}^{0.5}$ respectively. Subplots (b) and (c) present that total tensile elongation $A_5$ and area reduction $Z$ decrease linearly with $d_{Car}^{0.5}$, fitted as $A_5 = 64.5 - 6.445d_{Car}^{0.5}$ and $Z = 78 - 3.85d_{Car}^{0.5}$. Subplot (d) exhibits that Charpy impact toughness at -40°C drops sharply with increasing $d_{Car}^{0.5}$ in the region $d_{Car}^{0.5}<2$, and remains nearly 0 when $d_{Car}^{0.5}≥2$. The figure confirms that κ-carbide size is the dominant factor controlling the mechanical performance of the studied low-density steels: larger κ-carbide improves strength via shear strengthening (active even for 50 nm carbides) but significantly degrades ductility and low-temperature impact toughness, providing guidance for composition and process design of high-performance low-density steels.

This group of 4 subplots presents the tensile mechanical properties of solution-treated Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels, with solid lines representing true tensile stress-strain (SS) curves and dashed lines representing work hardening (WH) rate curves, with x-axis as true tensile strain (ε_T) and y-axis as stress/WH rate in MPa. Subplot (a) compares steels with 0.6-1.5wt% C after 1050℃ solution treatment, (b) compares steels with 20-35wt% Mn after 1050℃ solution treatment, (c) compares steels with 6-12wt% Al after 1050℃ solution treatment, (d) compares 6-12wt% Al steels after 1100℃ solution treatment. Key trends: WH rate increases with decreasing C, increasing Mn, and decreasing Al content, with peak values ~1900 MPa for 0.6C steel, ~2300 MPa for 35Mn steel, ~2100 MPa for 6Al steel treated at 1050℃. The true tensile strength (~1350 MPa) and fracture strength (~2150 MPa) are almost independent of alloying content and solution temperature. Except for 0.6C and 30Mn steels showing monotonic WH rate decrease with strain, other steels show a WH rate trend of rapid initial drop, slow rise, then final drop.
